coredata.dc:title: Degradation of antibiotic pollutants by persulfate activated with various carbon materials
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yuan
Wang, Qing
Ji, Guozhao
Li, Aimin
coredata.dc:description: Antibiotic residues are ubiquitous in wastewater, surface water, and groundwater worldwide with concentrations ranging from < 1 ng/L to hundreds μg/L, which pose ecological risks to aquatic organisms and humans. To date, the removal of antibiotic pollutants by advanced oxidation process (AOPs) has attracted tremendous attentions. The persulfate-based AOPs show higher oxidation, better selectivity, wider pH range, and no secondary pollution than the traditional Fenton-based AOPs. Carbon materials with low cost and chemical stability are useful for the activation of persulfate (Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and Peroxydisulfate (PDS)) to produce reactive species. However, there is lack of a comprehensive and critical review reporting the characteristics and influencing mechanism of various carbon materials (e.g. activated carbon, biochar, carbon nanotube, graphene, and carbon quantum dots, etc) and antibiotics (e.g. tetracycline, quinolone, β-lactam, sulfonamide, macrolide, etc) on the degradation behavior of antibiotic pollutants by the persulfate-based AOPs. Therefore, this study provided a comprehensive understanding on the degradation of antibiotics by carbon-activated persulfate system. Besides, the roles of operation parameters and synergistic effects of carbon materials combined with other assisted strategies are discussed systematically. Finally, the challenges and prospects for future investigation are proposed.

head.ce:title: Degradation of antibiotic pollutants by persulfate activated with various carbon materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Linggong Road 2, Dalian 116024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotic residues are ubiquitous in wastewater, surface water, and groundwater worldwide with concentrations ranging from < 1 ng/L to hundreds μg/L, which pose ecological risks to aquatic organisms and humans. To date, the removal of antibiotic pollutants by advanced oxidation process (AOPs) has attracted tremendous attentions. The persulfate-based AOPs show higher oxidation, better selectivity, wider pH range, and no secondary pollution than the traditional Fenton-based AOPs. Carbon materials with low cost and chemical stability are useful for the activation of persulfate (Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and Peroxydisulfate (PDS)) to produce reactive species. However, there is lack of a comprehensive and critical review reporting the characteristics and influencing mechanism of various carbon materials (e.g. activated carbon, biochar, carbon nanotube, graphene, and carbon quantum dots, etc) and antibiotics (e.g. tetracycline, quinolone, β-lactam, sulfonamide, macrolide, etc) on the degradation behavior of antibiotic pollutants by the persulfate-based AOPs. Therefore, this study provided a comprehensive understanding on the degradation of antibiotics by carbon-activated persulfate system. Besides, the roles of operation parameters and synergistic effects of carbon materials combined with other assisted strategies are discussed systematically. Finally, the challenges and prospects for future investigation are proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been widely used to promote animal growth and protect human health from infectious diseases [1,2]. According to their chemical structure, the common antibiotics include sulfonamide, tetracycline, fluoroquinolone, macrolide, and β-lactam [1,3]. Actually, many antibiotics are not fully metabolized by humans and animals, leading to their release into sewage treatment plants with urines and feces, and finally into the environment [1,3]. Between 2000 and 2015, the global consumption of antibiotics increased from 21.1 to 34.8 billion tones daily [3]. It has been estimated that the global consumption of antibiotics will increase about 67% by 2030, particularly in China, United States, India, Brazil, and Germany [3]. The abuse of antibiotics could lead to both environmental toxicity and antibiotic-resistant bacteria [2,3].

Currently, various treatment methods have been developed to remove antibiotics from wastewater, including adsorption, biological treatment, chemical treatment, etc. However, the adsorption can only separate antibiotics from wastewater, rather than complete degradation [1]. And the biological treatment has low removal efficiency due to the inhibitory effect of antibiotics to bacterial activity [4]. In contrast, advanced oxidation process (AOPs) can produce highly reactive radicals, such as hydroxyl radicals (HO•), sulfate radicals (SO4 •−) and superoxide radicals (O2 •−), which can degrade the antibiotics into low toxicity products [1,5,6]. AOPs can be commonly divided into five different types: ozone-based, ultraviolet-based, electrochemical-based, catalytic and physical AOPs [1]. Nevertheless, the traditional Fenton-AOPs are usually performed in an acidic condition, which limits its application in the practical industries [4,5]. Moreover, large amounts of iron-containing sludge are difficult to be disposed of [1,4,5]. Fortunately, the persulfate-based AOPs are promising alternative to address the above-mentioned problems [5]. The sulfate radical with high redox potential (E0 = 2.5–3.1 V) and strong oxidation capacity has been paid much more attentions [1]. The persulfate, including Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and Peroxydisulfate (PDS), can be activated to produce reactive radicals by heat, ultraviolet, ultrasonic, carbon materials, and transition metals oxides, ozone, and electricity [1,7,8]. Among them, carbon materials are relatively low-cost without any additional energy consumption, recyclable, and environmentally friendly. Many studies have been performed on the activation of persulfate by carbon materials to remove the antibiotic pollutants [9–12].

However, most of the reviews about the persulfate-based AOPs focus on either the degradation mechanism [13,14], or the removal of organic pollutants [14], not specific to antibiotics. It should be noticed that the types of antibiotics could lead to different reactions, intermediates and degradation pathways. Meanwhile, most of reviews only introduce the single carbon material, such as graphene [15], carbon nanotube [15], graphitic carbon nitride-based material [16], and metal-free carbon materials [17,18]. For example, a review by Xiao et al. reported the application of carbon materials in persulfate-based advanced oxidation processes. However, it is lack of common carbon materials, such as activated carbon and biochar. And the objective is not aimed at the removal of antibiotics [19].

According to the above information, three aspects can be concluded. (I) Antibiotic pollutants have been widely detected in wastewater, surface water, and groundwater, which pose ecological risks to aquatic organisms and humans. (II) Persulfate-based AOPs are promising strategy for the removal of antibiotic pollutants. (III) Carbon materials are low-cost and high-efficiency catalyst for persulfate-based AOPs. However, there is a lack of a comprehensive review about all kinds of carbon materials-activated persulfate AOPs for the degradation of various types of antibiotics. Therefore, the degradation mechanisms of antibiotics by persulfate-based AOPs activated with various carbon materials are summarized in this work, including adsorption process, free radical pathway, and nonradical pathway. Secondly, the characteristics of different carbon materials and the influences of these characteristics on the degradation behaviors of antibiotic are discussed. Especially, the roles of metal-doped and nonmetal-doped carbon materials in the degradation pathways are also investigated. Then, we also summarize the main attack sites of reactive oxygen species towards various types of antibiotics. Thirdly, the important operation parameters, such as pH, temperature, dosage, and coexisting ions, are concluded. Finally, the synergistic effects of carbon materials combined with other assisted strategies are systematically compared.

Degradation mechanisms of carbon-activated persulfate AOPs could be summarized into three aspects, including adsorption, free radical pathway and nonradical pathway [20]. More specifically, the nonradical pathway could be subdivided into singlet oxygen, electron transfer, and direct oxidation (Fig. 1 ). It should be noticed that the above-mentioned activation mechanisms often occur in a reaction simultaneously [21,22]. The activation of persulfate by carbon materials are affected by their surface functional groups, surface electrochemical impedance, graphitization degree and porous structure, which are related to the raw materials, preparation strategies, and modification methods. The identification of active sites and contribution of each reaction pathway to the total degradation performance have been discussed in many researches [4,10,23].

Adsorption of antibiotics by carbon materials is inevitable due to their porous structure and surface functional groups. The adsorption process normally includes three stages, namely external diffusion, particle diffusion, and surface reaction. The external diffusion, also called mass transfer, involves in the transfer of antibiotic molecules around the carbon materials. The antibiotic molecules penetrate the hydration film to the surface of carbon materials during the particle diffusion stage. The adsorption mechanisms include pore filling, surface complexation, electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, and π-π interaction [24]. The adsorption behavior could be affected by the types and amounts of surface functional groups on carbon materials, including acidic, alkaline, and neutral functional groups [25]. The adsorption process is also connected with average pore size and hierarchical porous structure of carbon materials. Additionally, it has been found that the adsorption of antibiotics and persulfate by carbon materials had a positively synergistic effect on the following advanced oxidation process [26,27]. More importantly, the adsorption of antibiotics by carbon materials is the rate-limiting step of the whole process [26,27].

Generally, three kinds of free radicals involve in the persulfate-based degradation system, namely SO4 •−, HO• and O2 •−. The radical quenching tests with methanol (SO4 •− and HO•) and tert-butyl alcohol (HO•) as well as spin trapping with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) are used to determine the roles of free radicals. SO4 •− is a strong oxidant with a standard redox potential of 2.5–3.1 V, which presents more excellent oxidation capacity than HO• (1.9–2.7 V). SO4 •− has a long half-life (30–40 μs), which is 30 times higher than that of HO•, contributing to full contact with antibiotics. SO4 •− can be applied in a much wider range of pH from 2 to 9. The unsaturated bonds and aromatic organic compounds with π electrons provide higher selectivity towards specific pollutants [14,28]. The active sites of carbon materials can catalyze the decomposition of persulfate to produce sulfate radicals (Eqs (1)-(6)). The species of free radicals could be changed with the solution pH. The main active species was SO4 •− in the pH range from 2 to 7. The coexistence of SO4 •− and HO• normally occur in the pH range from 7 to 10. SO4 •− could react with −OH to form HO•, which is also responsible for the degradation of pollutants (Eq (7)) [13,29]. O2 •− could be produced as an intermediate product in the presence of dissolved oxygen, during which O2 •− can recombine to form 1O2 by various ways in different conditions (Eqs (8)–(11)) [28,30].

Recently, the nonradical pathway has also been found in the persulfate-based AOPs, which can be divided into singlet oxygen, electron transfer, and direct oxidation. Compared with radical pathway, the nonradical pathway can be used in a much larger pH range, including acidic, neutral, and basic condition. Additionally, the selectivity and redox potential of the nonradical pathway improve their resistance to different inorganic ions, halogens, and background organics [31–33]. Thirdly, the oxidation capacity of persulfate could be fully developed in the nonradical pathway. Last but not least, the self-quenching of the generated radicals could be avoided during the nonradical pathway [25].

Singlet oxygen (1O2) is the most important oxidation species with high selectivity in the nonradical pathway [34]. Chemical quenching with sodium azide or L-histidine, chemical trapping with diphenylanthracene by high performance liquid chromatography, and spin trapping with 2,2,6,6,-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol by EPR can be used to detect 1O2. Generally, 1O2 can be produced in two different ways. On the one hand, 1O2 can be produced in the self-degradation stage of both PMS and PDS (Eqs (12)-(15)) [28]. But the process is relatively slow (k = 4.7–5.9 × 10−2 M−1 s−1) [28]. The accumulation of quinones can enhance the production of 1O2 from PMS self-degradation [35]. On the other hand, the ketonic groups in p-benzoquinone and cyclohexanone can also promote the production of 1O2 in an alkaline condition [32]. The carbonyl/ketonic groups could serve as active sites for the production of 1O2. However, the overabundant carbonyl groups with electronegativity might disturb the conjugated π system and decrease the zeta potential of carbon materials, leading to negative influence on AOPs [36]. Ntzoufra et al. also found the overabundant carbonyl groups by long-time pretreatment of sodium persulfate under reflux could reduce the catalytic activity of biochar [37]. Moreover, many investigations indicate that 1O2 produced by the self-degradation of PDS and PMS can promote the persulfate-based AOPs in both acidic and neutral conditions [38,39]. Interestingly, an alternative mechanism involving in the hydrolysis of PDS catalyzed by carbon nanotube was proposed [28].

1O2 is a highly selective oxidant, which can react with almost all the unsaturated organics via electrophilic addition and electron abstraction. Phenolic compounds with electron-donating substituents can form radical cations with the intact benzene rings or phenoxyl radicals via electron transfer to 1O2. These radicals can be oxidized by the ground-state oxygen, rearranged to dioxetanes, and finally yielded quinone-like and ring-opening products [28]. However, the reaction kinetic rates of 1O2 with most of the antibiotic pollutants are slower than those of radical reactions. The kinetic rates of 1O2 is significantly lower than those of SO4 •− and HO• [28]. Therefore, the application of 1O2-based nonradical pathway in the practical industries should be paid more attention. And multi-strategies and critical methods should be performed to determine the particular role of 1O2 [40]. Besides, the carbonyl groups and vicinal hydroxyl functionalities in the natural organic matters could also promote the generation of 1O2, which was favorable for the application of nonradical pathway for the in-situ chemical oxidation in the practical rivers, lakes, and underground waters [25].

During the electron transfer process, the pollutant is electron donor, the carbon-based catalyst is transmission medium, and the persulfate is electron acceptor [41,42]. Impedance spectroscopy, linear sweep voltammetry and substrate specific reaction are used to investigate the existence of surface electron transfer [10,23,43]. Carbon materials with low electrochemical impedance and good conductivity not only delivery electron transfer directly, but also produce some active substances as electronic medium to activate persulfate. This is a typical oxidation mechanism of the nonradical pathway by the activation of carbon materials in the persulfate-based AOPs, which is highly related to the electron hole, porous structure, and charge transfer complex [10,23,43,44]. Furthermore, the graphitized porous structures of carbon materials not only provide activate sites for electron holes, but also possess strong electron reception ability with the aromatic pollutants through the conjugated π electron system. The surface electron transfer complexes have low electrochemical impedance, which can act as electron spinders for the direct charge transfer. Notably, the electron transfer efficiency is an important factor in the nonradical pathway. The aromatic structure and graphitized structure of carbon materials are the media of electron shuttle [28,45,46]. Modification of carbon materials can reduce the surface resistivity and enhance the electron transfer efficiency [22,47]. Huo et al. synthesized N/S co-doped porous carbon using coffee ground, sodium bicarbonate and L-cysteine monohydrochloride as precursors [47]. The results indicated that the excellent degradation efficiency for tetracycline hydrochloride was ascribed to the electron transfer and 1O2 due to the N/S doping. And the carbonyl groups served as the main active sites to promote the electron transfer [47].

Similarly, the electron transfer was also observed in Qi's study [22]. A three-dimensional graphene-like biochar derived from Enteromorpha was used as a PDS activator for the degradation of sulfamethoxazole. Interestingly, the study confirmed that the formation of sp2-hybrid carbon was crucial to the electron transfer on the surface of biochar, because the electron transfer could be enhanced by the graphitic carbon defects formed in the sp2-hybrid carbon network. Besides, the graphitic N could accelerate the electron transfer between the adjacent carbon atoms by breaking the intrinsic conjugated graphitic network. The introduction of graphitic N increased the amounts of adjacent carbon atoms with positive charges, which promoted the weakening of OO bonds to form surfaced metastable PDS by electron rearrangement. The formed metastable reaction complexes could improve the interaction between persulfate and N-biochar via electron transfer. 1O2 could also be generated through the nucleophilic addition reaction of PDS with positively charged carbon. The crucial degradation mechanism of sulfamethoxazole by N-doped biochar involved surface electron transfer, 1O2 together with O2 •− radicals [22,48].

Persulfate can oxidize the antibiotics without involving any radicals [48,49]. Elbs oxidation and Boyland-Sims oxidation are the primary mechanisms in most PDS cases, leading to the hydroxylation of phenols and arylamines [28]. In Chen's work, β-lactam antibiotics were transferred to PMS by double electron transfer directly, resulting in the breakage of peroxide bond and selective self-degradation [48]. PMS could also oxidize sulfonamide antibiotics directly by selectively attacking the N and S atoms on the benzene ring [49]. However, the direct oxidation of antibiotic pollutants by persulfate suffers from two shortcomings. The direct oxidation capacity of persulfate is weak. Additionally, the direct oxidation process can be only applied to the pollutants with electron-rich groups, such as amino and hydroxyl groups. Consequently, the direct oxidation process normally combines with other degradation pathways, such as adsorption, free radical pathway, and 1O2.

Activated carbon (AC) is one of the most widely-used nonmetal catalyst for persulfate-based AOPs owing to its well-developed porous structure, large specific surface area, abundant surface functional groups, and low cost [12,50–52]. Four active sites of AC mainly contributed to the activation of persulfate, including surface functional groups, delocalized π electrons, sp2 hybrid carbon at defect edges and oxygen-containing functional groups (COOH, COH, CO) [12,14,51,52]. These active sites can react with the persulfate to produce SO4 •− and HO• for the removal of antibiotics [13,53].The types of AC for persulfate activation are Powder activated carbon (PAC) [54], Granular activated carbon (GAC) [55], and Activated carbon fiber (ACF) [54]. Among them, PAC has much higher specific surface area and better adsorption performance for the antibiotics. However, it is difficult to recycle PAC from the solution and reuse repeatedly. Therefore, some studies have been investigated on the preparation of magnetic AC for the activation of persulfate [50,56–58].

Another drawback for AC is the poor mass transfer of pollutant molecules inside the deep pores due to the bottleneck of micropores, thus limiting the degradation rate. In other words, the catalytic reaction only occurs on the carbon surface or the adjacent boundary layer. This is the reason why AC possesses large specific surface area, but poor performance. This problem can be solved by casting the porous structure with mesoporous or hierarchical pores through soft/hard template method [59]. The roles of various carbon materials in the persulfate-based AOPs are different. Pham et al. used GAC and CNT as the activator for persulfate to degrade acetaminophen [51]. The results show that the surface functional groups and specific surface area of GAC were larger than those of CNT. However, the pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant (kobs) of CNT system was 50 times higher than that of GAC. It was due to the sp2 hybrid carbon sheet and oxygen functional groups of CNT contributed to better performance than those of GAC. More interestingly, it was found that the free radical pathway was dominant in the GAC-PDS system, while the nonradical pathway was dominant in the CNT-PDS system. Liang et al. compare the degradation behaviors of activated carbon and biochar [53]. The degradation efficiency of sulfamethoxazole was 91.2%, 88.7%, and 30.4% for commercial AC, BC-700 °C, and BC-300 °C, which was attributed to best adsorption ability of commercial AC with the highest specific surface area of 950 m2/g. More specifically, SO4 •− and HO• radicals in solid phase of commercial AC contributed to high removal performance but incomplete degradation. However, the main degradation by BC occurred in solution with SO4 •− as the major reaction species (53.1%).

Biochar (BC) is produced by the pyrolysis of biomass wastes under the oxygen-free or oxygen-limited conditions from 300 to 800 °C [13,60]. The common biochar includes sludge-based biochar [45,61], straw-based biochar [62,63], wood-based biochar [64,65], algae-based biochar [22,66], and shell-based biochar [53,67]. The remarkable difference between BC and AC is their specific surface area. Generally, BC is produced by direct carbonization of biomass wastes without any chemical or physical activating agents, such as KOH, H3PO4, ZnCl2, CO2, and water vapor. Therefore, BC normally possess low specific surface area (<500 m2/g). Moreover, AC can also be obtained by using coal-based, plastic-based, and pitch-based precursors, not only biomass wastes. Similar properties between BC and AC are rich functional groups and low cost [13,68]. Therefore, BC is also used as a catalyst for the activation of persulfate in recent five years [64,69,70].

The oxygen functional groups and persistent free radicals on the surface of BC were important for the persulfate activation [64]. Wang et al. proposed the possible activation mechanisms of persulfate by BC [68]. The persulfate activation was ascribed to the formed defective structures and persistent free radicals in BC, including semiquinones and phenoxyls. Additionally, some minerals in precursors could be retained in the form of stable mineral crystals after pyrolysis for following persulfate activation process [68]. The activation of persulfate could also be promoted due to high graphitization degree and aromatic conjugated π electrons in BC [14,22,68].

High reaction activity of carbon nanotube (CNT) is attributed to its good conductivity, high-level electron mobility, and low mass-transfer resistance [15]. The various dimensionalities and surface chemistry of CNT (1D) are responsible for the different catalytic performance compared to other carbon materials, e.g. graphene (2D) and hierarchical AC (3D). The catalytic reactivity of CNT is related to the defect level and electron state, which could be changed by increasing wall layers and doping heteroatoms [11,15,71–73]. For example, a Fe3C encapsulated N-doped CNT could activate the persulfate to degrade sulfamethoxazole via hydroxylation, oxidation, ring opening, bond cleavage, and desulfonation, during which the nonradical 1O2 was the crucial pathway [73]. Duan et al. found that catalytic performance of persulfate by CNT could be enhanced by N incorporation due to higher adsorption energy and faster charge transfer [72].

CNT can be divided into single-walled CNT (SWCNT) and multi-walled CNT (MWCNT). Generally, the surface of MWCNT is much more active than SWCNT due to the existence of large numbers of surface functional groups, such as COOH, CO, and OH. Guan et al. investigated the oxidation of sulfamethoxazole by PDS activated with MWCNT [71]. The degradation efficiency of sulfamethoxazole in MWCNT system was ten times higher than that in zero-valent iron system. Six degradation intermediate products were produced via hydroxylation, opening of isoxazole ring, and cleavage of sulfonamide N-S bond. The aniline moieties in sulfamethoxazole were vulnerable to attack by the nonradical reactive species.

Graphene is a thin carbon sheet with a six-member carbon ring as the skeleton and various types of edge defects. The surface functional groups of graphene include hydroxyl (OH), carboxyl (COOH), carbonyl (CO) and epoxy groups (COC) [74]. The graphene materials can be divided into pristine graphene, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and graphene oxide (GO) based on the degree of surface oxidation. Generally, the GO/rGO with acidic surface is required to modify into basic surface for persulfate activation [75,76]. The modified graphene are good candidates for persulfate catalysis due to the following reasons. The specific surface area is higher than that of CNT and BC for the adsorption and catalysis process. The good electrical conductivity and electron mobility for the redox reaction are derived from the high density sp2 electrons of graphene [77,78]. For example, the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of rGO were removed by a hydrothermal method, which could improve the integrity of π-conjugated structure and surface hydrophobicity [79,80]. Chen et al. found that the adsorbed amount of rGO for the aromatic nitro compounds was enhanced approximately 10–50 times than that of commercial CNT via controlling the amounts of surface oxygen-containing groups [80]. After reduction treatment, the chemical activity of rGO was improved. However, the surface active sites were blocked due to the agglomeration at high reduction degree, reducing the catalytic and adsorption performance [81]. The surface ketonic group on rGO could react with PMS to produce SO4 •− via electron transfer. The functional groups with abundant electrons, such as nucleophilic ketonic and quinoidic groups, could serve as active sites for the redox reactions [82]. Notably, the activation of persulfate by graphene suffers from two obvious drawbacks. Firstly, graphene is more expensive than other carbon materials [13]. Secondly, the classical Hummers synthesis method of GO involves in many dangerous oxidizing reagents, such as KMnO4, concentrated HNO3 and H2SO4.

Some scholars also investigated other carbon materials for the activation of persulfate, such as acetylene carbon black [83], fractionated porous carbon [59], mesoporous carbon [4,84] and carbon quantum dots [85]. The applications of these carbon materials to persulfate-based AOPs could produce excellent performance due to their good light stability and well-defined porous structure [4,59,85,86]. Carbon quantum dots with high quantum efficiency, good biocompatibility, and light stability have received a lot of attention for photocatalysis [87]. A large number of electron-hole pairs can be generated during the photocatalysis [88]. Peng et al. conducted a photocatalysis process for the degradation of tetracycline by persulfate activated with N/P co-doped graphite carbon nitride and carbon quantum dots (Fig. 2 ) [85]. The free radical of SO4 •− and HO• were dominant species in the system with persulfate. But h+, O2 •−, and HO• were key reactive species in photocatalysis without persulfate. The hierarchical porous carbons with high surface area, well-defined porosity, tunable surface chemistry, and excellent chemical stability are beneficial for activation of persulfate [89,90]. In Tian’s work, the N/P doped hierarchical porous carbons were highly efficient for the degradation of sulfachloropyridazine with a degradation efficiency of 100% within only 20 min at a catalyst loading of 0.05 g/L, potassium persulfate dosage of 6.5 mM, and initial pollutant concentration of 20 mg/L [59].

The pristine carbon materials may suffer from poor cycling performance, low removal efficiency, and high dosage [13]. Besides, the degradation mechanisms of antibiotics by persulfate activated with the pristine carbon materials are mainly free radical pathway, which are less efficient than nonradical pathway [91]. Therefore, many researchers doped carbon materials with nonmetal and metal elements [14,61,68,92].

The nonmetal elements, such as nitrogen (N), sulphur (S), phosphorus (P), and boron (B), are widely used for the modification of carbon materials. More interestingly, the nonmetal doping is most common in biochar. It is due to the in-situ existence of these elements in the raw precursors, such as straw [62,63], algae [22,66,93], sludge [68,94], bamboo [65], coffee ground [47,95], and so on [96–98]. Many previous studies demonstrated that the catalytic activity of carbon materials could be enhanced by doping nonmetal elements [47,59,96,97,99,100]. Duan et al. unveiled that only a small amount of N-doping (0.80%) in CNT could enhance the degradation rate about 57-fold [101]. Duan et al. also reported that the catalytic performance of S/N co-doped rGO and N-doped SWCNT was 3 times and 2 times higher than that of undoped-sample [11,102].

Furthermore, the degradation mechanism could be also changed from free radical pathway to nonradical pathway after nonmetal doping. Duan found that the N-doping in CNT transformed the dominated degradation pathway from a free radical pathway to a nonradical pathway [101]. This was because the doped-nitrogen atom had a higher electronegativity than carbon atom, which could attract electrons from the adjacent carbon atoms, leading to the positively charged carbon atoms [102,103]. Afterwards, the active carbon atoms would interact with the persulfate to form a highly reactive species via a direct electron abstraction without producing any sulfate/hydroxyl radicals. However, Liang et al. detected the existence of 1O2 and a small amount of sulfate and hydroxyl radicals in the N-doped graphene activated-persulfate system [39]. Wang et al. also found the similar reaction pathway in the system of ferric carbide encapsulated N-doped CNT [40]. Briefly, the dominated mechanism for the nonmetal-doped carbon materials activated-persulfate AOPs is still nonradical pathway.

According to the researches [104,105], the carbon materials doped with the nonmetal elements could (i) increase the defective edges, (ii) improve the π-electron mobility through conjugation, (iii) change the electron density in the local carbon atoms, (iv) introduce new electrocatalytic active sites and functionalities for selective adsorption and degradation, (v) enhance the surface hydrophilicity, and (vi) accelerate the electron transfer reaction with the persulfate. All of these characteristics are helpful to improve the degradation efficiency of antibiotics. Both doping amount and heteroatom species are important for the catalysis performance, which should be noticed in the practical applications. Generally, the catalysis performance will be improved by increasing the doping amounts in a certain range. However, the reduction of degradation could be caused by the over-dosage of nonmetal elements in the carbon materials. In the Frank’s study, the over-doped of B2O3 in graphitic carbon leaded to the block of the zig–zag defects directly, and the decrease of degradation efficiency [106].

Cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), iron (Fe), aluminum (Al), and manganese (Mn) are used for the modification of carbon materials in the form of single metal, metal oxide, multiple metal oxide, and single atom [107]. Among them, the carbon-based magnetic nanocomposites with different compositions and phase states have attracted much more attention, including iron oxides (Fe3O4, γ-Fe2O3), single metals (Fe, Co), spinel-type ferromagnetic materials (MgFe2O4, MnFe2O4, and CoFe2O4,), and some alloys (CoPt3, FePt) [56,108–110]. The carbon-based magnetic nanocomposites can be separated from the solutions rapidly and easily. More importantly, the persulfate can be activated by the carbon-based magnetic nanocomposites through a Mn+/Mn+1 (M = Fe, Cu, Co, Mn) electron recycling process (Eqs (16)-(17)) [56,108–110]. Moreover, the carbon-metal nanocomposites could form new configuration of atoms, which may reduce the local function of outer carbon surface and the resistance for the activation of persulfate through electrostatic and covalent interactions. The FeC bond is more beneficial for the transfer of electrons from the metal to persulfate in compared with FeO bond [56].

Sewage sludge not only contains organics, water, nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, but also many metal elements, such as Fe, Cu, Zn, and Ni. Therefore, the metal-doped biochar could be produced from sewage sludge without any additional chemicals, which provides a feasible approach for the reuse of sewage sludge [45,57,68,94,97,100]. In Yin’s work, the removal rate of sulfamethoxazole by sludge-derived biochar in PDS system reached 94.6%, and the degradation process followed a nonradical pathway [94]. However, Jia et al. found the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride preferred a free radical pathway with a removal rate of 99.72% at a sodium persulfate dosage of 5 mM, Fe-doped carbon material loading of 0.2 g/L, initial tetracycline hydrochloride of 60 mg/L, and pH of 4 [97]. The above-mentioned results indicate that the degradation pathway of the metal-doped carbon material-based catalytic system is complicated. The degradation behavior is dependent on their valence states, particle size, morphology, crystalline structure, and grain boundary, which subsequently determine the redox potential and binding energy between metal sites and persulfate.

The types of antibiotics, such as tetracycline, quinolone, β-lactam, sulfonamide, macrolide, metronidazole, and ketoprofen, have a great influence on their degradation pathways and mechanisms during the carbon materials-activated persulfate AOPs [111]. Although the degradation pathway of each system is different, some similar patterns could also provide a direction to understand the influences of antibiotic types on the overall degradation process, and how to remove these antibiotics more effectively. The radical quenching EPR and chronoamperometry test are usually used to determine the degradation behavior. Moreover, gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy, and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy coupled with density functional theory are applied to determine the intermediates and degradation pathway of the antibiotics [1,4,23,67,73,97,112,113].

Tetracycline antibiotics consist of a planar polycyclic structure with four hydrocarbon rings, which can be subdivided into tetracycline, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, and doxycycline [1,2,84]. Tetracycline antibiotics can be transformed into the intermediates by opening the rings during the persulfate-based AOPs. Then, these intermediates are further cracked and oxidized into small molecules. Finally, the tetracycline antibiotics are completely mineralized into carbon dioxide and water molecule via cracking and oxidation. The attack of CC, CC, and CN bond in tetracycline antibiotics by the active oxygen species, including SO4 •− and HO•, 1O2, and electron transfer, contribute to the hydroxylation, demethylation, and decarboxylation [67,112–114]. Pi et al. used Fe3O4-doped biochar to activate persulfate for the degradation of tetracycline [67]. The oxygen-containing functional groups and Fe3O4 nanoparticles increased the removal rate of tetracycline to 92.3%. Briefly, tetracycline was degraded through dehydration, oxidation of double bonds and aromatic ring, loss of dimethyl amino, and cleavage of carbamic ring (Fig. 3 ). Initially, HO• attacked the C11a-C12 and C2-C3 double bond to produce intermediate A. SO4 •− oxidized the C4 position of dimethyl amino group with a low bond energy to form the product C. The aromatic rings were attacked to form product C, for example C6a-C7 bond. The open of benzene ring resulted in a ketonic group at the C6a position and a carboxylic group at the C7 position (product F). Secondly, the product B, D and E were created via the similar process by the loss of dimethyl amino at the C4 position, oxidation of double bonds at the C11a-C12 position, and oxidation of aromatic ring at the C6a-C7 position, respectively. Moreover, the product D and E could also form the product C and F via the oxidation of double bonds at the C2-C3 position. Afterwards, the product G was produced from product B through the dehydration pathway at C6 position. Product G could further transform to the product I owing to the cleavage of the carbon atomic ring by the oxidation of SO4 •−. Finally, the intermediates were degraded into some smaller substances and completely decomposed into H2O and CO2.

Quinolone antibiotics are one of the most widely used broad-spectrum antibiotics with a basic structure of 4-quinolone [13,66]. Quinolone antibiotics can be subdivided into norfloxacin [57], flumequine [29], ofloxacin [44,93], and ciprofloxacin [66]. The quinolone rings are opened by the attack of reactive oxygen species. Then, the quinolone molecules were transformed into smaller intermediates through carbonylation, decarboxylation, hydroxylation, and final mineralization to CO2 and H2O.

A novel heterogeneous catalyst, namely polyhydroquinone-coated magnetite/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Fe3O4/MWCNT/PHQ), was used to activate PDS for the removal of flumequine [29]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the CH bond at 7C position was firstly attacked to form the hydroxylated flumequine (P1). With the reaction proceeding, two major degradation pathways were found. Pathway I was initiated by the substitution of fluorine by a hydroxyl group to form P2, followed by opening the right ring through the breaking of the 2C 3C double bonds to form P3. Afterwards, the decarbonylation, decarboxylation and hydroxylation occurred in sequence to form the corresponding intermediates P4, P5 and P6. During pathway II, P7 was produced by breaking the 2C 3C double bonds of P1. Subsequently, the reaction proceeded by the similar patterns to form P8 (decarbonylation) and P9 (decarboxylation), which further reacted with SO4 •− and HO• to generate P10 (demethylation) and P13 (decarbonylation). Then, P11 was produced from P10 by the hydroxylation and continued to form P12 by ring opening.

β-lactam antibiotics include two types, namely penicillin and cephalosporin [115]. Cephalosporin has stronger stability than penicillin due to its smaller ring tension [23]. Penicillin antibiotics can be subdivided into ampicillin, oxacillin, and cloxacillin, while cephalosporin antibiotics can be subdivided into cephalexin and cephadroxyl [11,116]. The dominated regions of the electrophilic attack towards oxacillin and cephalexin by persulfate were simulated by DFT [116]. The thiazide and β-lactam rings of cephalexin and the thiazole and β-lactam rings of oxacillin with high electron density were attacked and oxidized by the reactive oxygen species. The OCNC structure between the thiophene ring and β-lactam ring were the most fragile bond, which could be attacked to form the intermediates [10,11,23]. The branched-chains of thiazine and β-lactam rings were mineralized by the oxygen-containing groups of carbon materials, especially the carboxyl groups. Besides, the reactive oxygen species, including hydroxyl and sulfate radicals, could also break the side chains [23,117]. Finally, the resultant by-products were further oxidized by the reactive oxygen species into CO2, NH4 +, and H2O [11,23,116].

Sulfonamide antibiotics with a structure of p-aminophenol phthalamide can be subdivided into sulfamethoxazole, sulfadiazine, and sulfadimethyrimidine [115]. Sulfamethoxazole is selected as an example to summarize the degradation route of sulfonamide antibiotics [22]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the sulfate and hydroxyl radicals preferred to attack the SN bond of sulfamethoxazole. Afterwards, 3-methyl-5-amino-isoxazole and 4-aminobenzene sulfonic acid were formed. Then, the isoxazole rings were oxidized to open the rings, and further degraded into CO2 and H2O through deamination, desulfurization, and bond splitting. However, 1O2 was more likely to attack the 11 N atom on the benzene ring in a nonradical pathway. 1O2 with an unoccupied π2p molecular orbital has strong electrophilicity [94]. According to the DFT calculation, the highest fA − value of sulfamethoxazole was located at the 11 N atom. More specifically, the 11 N atom of the aniline ring was the most vulnerable site to be attacked by 1O2. The parameter fA − was the Fukui function value of the atom, which was used to evaluate the oxidation of the electrophilic agent [94]. The 1C, 3C, and 5C atoms with high activities could also be attacked by 1O2. The attack of amino group on the benzene ring resulted in the transformation of aniline, and the cleavage or hydroxylation of the benzene ring for the following degradation and oxidation into CO2 and H2O. It should be noticed that the degradation pathways of sulfonamide antibiotics were different in various carbon materials and catalytic systems [22,63,73,94,118].

The degradation of other antibiotics by AOPs could also be found in the literatures, such as macrolide [119], metronidazole [120], and ketoprofen [119]. However, the investigations on the removal of these antibiotic pollutants by persulfate-based AOPs with carbon materials as the activator are relatively rare, which should be performed in the future. Fig. 6 displays the degradation of metronidazole with an efficiency of 94.5% in 60 min using a ternary composite of g-C3N4, graphene and MnFe2O4 as activator for persulfate under visible light [1,121]. Firstly, the p–n heterojunction between g-C3N4 and MnFe2O4 played a critical role in accelerating the separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, while the graphene served as a charge channel for the rapid transfer of charges. Then, more active species (h+, O2 •− and HO•) were generated under visible light via primary reactions. Secondly, the favorable self-redox cycles Fe and Mn sites on the catalyst surface could activate PDS to produce additional reactive oxygen species, for example SO4 •−. However, O2 •− and h+ were proved as the dominant reactive species for metronidazole removal. Therefore, PDS activation process only played an auxiliary role for metronidazole removal in this system [121].

The solution pH can affect the reactive oxygen species, surface charges of the carbon materials and dissociation morphologies of the target antibiotics [13,14,29,57,84]. Firstly, the species of free radicals are related with the solution pH (Fig. 7 ). The main active species is SO4 •− at pH of 2–7. SO4 •− and HO• radicals coexist in solution at pH of 7–10. HO• radicals are responsible for the degradation of antibiotics above pH of 10. This is due to the fact that SO4 •− radicals can react with the OH to create the HO• radicals (Eq (7)) [13,29]. The decomposition rate (kobs) of PDS barely change in the neutral pH and weakly alkaline solution (3 < pH < 13), but increases in acidic (pH < 3) and strongly alkaline solution (pH > 13) [14,122,123]. Secondly, the carbon surface has a stronger affinity for anionic pollutants, when the solution pH is lower than zero potential of carbon materials (pHpzc). Furthermore, the solution pH also affects the surface complexation of persulfate [124]. Thirdly, the solution pH is closely related to the dissociation forms and deprotonation degrees of antibiotics. For example, the two-stage dissociation equilibrium constants of sulfamoxazole are at pKa1 of 1.6 and pKa2 of 5.7. Thus, the sulfamoxazole is dissociated at pH > 5.7 completely, during which the sulfamoxazole can be degraded by the reactive oxygen species easily [125,126]. Additionally, it should be noticed that the addition of persulfate could also lower the solution pH due to the release of H+ from persulfate decomposition and the presence of acidic bisulfate in oxone salt [13,14,84,123].

Remarkably, the relationship between solution pH and degradation performance is depended on the carbon materials, antibiotics, and persulfate. Therefore, the optimal pH ranges are different in various systems [12,29,84,114]. Feng et al. discussed the flumequine degradation behavior within the pH range of 3–11 in a MWCNT/PDS system [29]. The rapid flumequine degradation was obtained in the neutral and acidic pH range with the maximum removal efficiency at pH of 3. Jiang et al. found the best removal efficiency of tetracycline was obtained at the initial pH of 5 by a Fe0 immobilized mesoporous carbon [84]. The release of Fe2+ ions by the corrosion of Fe0 in an acidic condition could activate the persulfate to produce radicals. Besides, Huang et al. found that the degradation efficiency in acidic and alkaline condition was higher than that in neutral condition [93]. The involved mechanisms were concluded as follows. (I) 1O2 was promoted in acidic condition; (II) The dissolved metal ions and opened internal channels of carbon materials occurred in acidic condition; () The spontaneous decomposition of PMS under alkaline conditions could also produce 1O2. (IV) The excess OH in the alkaline solution provided more electrons.

The increase of solution temperature could improve the degradation efficiency and rate of antibiotics in the carbon materials-activated persulfate AOPs [13,29,44,59,66,83,127]. Zou et al. reported that kobs increased from 0.252 to 0.864 L/(mg·min) with the temperature increasing from 20 to 50 ℃ [66]. In Liu’s study, kobs of ofloxacin increased by 28 times from 30 to 60 ℃ [44]. Moreover, the increase of solution temperature could reduce the dosage of carbon-based catalyst by improving the degradation efficiency. For example, the dosage of porous carbon reduced to 2.5 mg/L at 45 ℃ [59].

Actually, heating itself is also a method for the activation of persulfate. The dynamic and thermodynamic performance can be promoted due to the synergistic contribution of heating and carbon materials [40,127]. Firstly, the increase of solution temperature could accelerate the decomposition rate of S2O8 2− and HSO5 − into free radicals [13,44]. Secondly, the increase of solution temperature could enhance the chemisorption of the antibiotic pollutants and PDS/PMS by carbon material [40]. Thirdly, the increase of solution temperature could promote the electron transfer between the antibiotic pollutants and persulfate through the carbon catalyst [40]. The calculated activation energy (Ea, kJ/mol) based on the Arrhenius equation are widely applied to evaluate the degradation process. The degradation of sulfamethoxazole was mainly due to the natural chemical reaction on the catalyst surface rather than the mass transfer efficiency by comparing the Ea values. More importantly, the energy consumption should be taken into consideration in spite of good degradation property at high temperature. For example, 35 ℃ was chosen as a cost-effective reaction temperature, even though the best performance was obtained at 45 ℃ [29].

The dosage of carbon materials and persulfate can affect the degradation behaviors of antibiotics [26,29,66,100,114]. Generally, increasing the dosage of carbon materials could improve the degradation efficiency at the single-factor condition, during which the other parameters are fixed. In Wang’s work, the removal rate of norfloxacin and kobs enhanced from 71.81% to 99.57% and 0.004 to 0.078 min−1, when the dosage of corn stalk-derived biochar increased from 0.2 to 3.2 g/L [100]. Meanwhile, the mineralization degree of norfloxacin was also improved significantly. In Feng’s research, the removal rate of total organic carbon increased from 15.2% to 68.1%, while the amount of carbon-based catalyst increased from 20 to 100 mg/L [29]. Actually, the increase of carbon dosage provides more active sites for the reaction of persulfate and antibiotics, resulting in high degradation efficiency and degradation rate. Besides, the increase of the catalyst dosage also improves the adsorbed amount for the following degradation process. Interestingly, Kakavandia et al. found that both the degradation efficiency and degradation rate reduced with the catalyst dosage increasing from 0.4 to 0.5 g/L [58]. This may be because the main mechanism under ultrasound irradiation was homogenous radical pathway, which was inhibited by high dosages of AC catalyst.

The degradation performance could also be improved by increasing the persulfate dosage. Shen et al. found the removal rate increased from 0.75% to 97.74% with the concentration of PMS increasing from 0 to 100 mg/L [113]. This was attributed to that the available reactive species were produced to combine with carbon materials and create more reactive oxygen species. Unfortunately, the degradation performance becomes poor with over-dosed persulfate [9,10]. Because the excessive persulfate may lead to the scavenge of the sulfate radicals into the persulfate radicals (S2O8 2−), which has much lower oxidizing ability than the sulfate radicals (Eqs (18), (19)) [66,120]. More interestingly, simple increasing the dosage of carbon materials-based catalyst is not useful due to the lack of comparable free radicals and reactive species through the interactions of carbon catalyst and persulfate oxidant dosage. Likewise, simply increasing the persulfate dosage without changing the dosage of carbon-based catalyst is also unavailable owing to the lack of enough active sites [93]. The change of carbon-based catalyst dosage is not linearly equivalent to that of persulfate dosage. Chen et al. proved that the increase of N-doped graphene dosage improved the degradation of antibiotics about 4 times, which was higher than that of increasing the PMS dosage [128]. This result indicates the carbon materials could provide better degradation efficiency than PMS at the same increasing dosage. Consequently, a more economical strategy for the improvement of degradation performance is increasing carbon-based catalyst dosage rather than PMS dosage.

The degradation behaviors of antibiotics are affected by the halogen and oxygen-containing anions in wastewater, including Cl−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, and NO2 − [9,12,28,73]. The coexisting anions have a greater influence on the free radical pathway than the nonradical pathway [112]. The halogen free radicals, especially Cl−, could scavenge SO4 •− or HO• and reduce their degradation efficiency based on the Eqs (20)–(25) [14,28,112]. The effect of halogen anions is more significant at high concentrations, such as saline groundwater, saline wastewater, and reverse osmosis concentrates [28]. A series of halogen radicals with higher standard reduction potential than SO4 •− and HO• could be formed, including X•, X2 •−, XY•−, and OX•− [28,129]. Consequently, the degradation efficiency reduces, especially for target compounds with low halogen activity. However, the antibiotics with high halogen activity could be oxidized easily by halogen ions than by SO4 •− and HO• [28,130]. Notably, the presence of halogen anions could produce toxic halogen by-products during the persulfate-based AOPs, for example trihalomethane [28,131]. Besides, the coexistence of Cl− could change the degradation mechanism of antibiotics (Fig. 8 ) [132].

The common oxygen-containing anions in the antibiotic wastewater include HPO4 −, H2PO4 2−, HCO3 −, and CO3 2− [28,133]. The influences of oxygen-containing anions on persulfate-based AOPs are summarized as follows. Firstly, the oxygen-containing anions can scavenge SO4 •− radicals and reduce the overall degradation efficiency [28,133,134]. The scavenging effects towards free radicals are more obvious, while the solution pH values exceed pKa of the corresponding anion acid [28,133]. Secondly, the weak oxidants (e.g., E0 (CO3 •−/CO3 2−) = +1.63 V) are highly selective for the electron-rich antibiotics [135]. Finally, the peroxide bond of PMS can be activated directly due to the nucleophilic attack of some anions, for example HCO3 − and HPO4 2− [28] .

The activation of persulfate by carbon materials can be enhanced by adding ultrasound in the frequency range of 20–1000 kHz. In a study by Kakavandi et al., the degradation efficiency of tetracycline increased from 36.8% to 38.5% with the assistance of ultrasonic [58]. Honestly, ultrasound itself is also an activation method for persulfate (Eqs (26), (27)). Three main mechanisms involve in the activation by carbon materials under ultrasound, namely sonoluminescence, hot spot, and oxygen atom escape [136,137]. In the sonoluminescence theory, the ultrasound irradiation generates visible light irradiation, which stimulates the carbon materials to form sono-induced carriers, and then electron-hole pairs (Eq (28)). In the “hot spot” theory, the generated thermal energy by cavitation effect could decompose water molecules into HO• and other free radicals (Eqs (29)–(34)) [58]. Additionally, the acoustic catalyst could be excited by the generated heat from the hot spot to produce electron-hole pairs, which could form additional free radicals [58]. The enhancement of electron transfer could accelerate the generation of SO4 •− radicals, thus improving the degradation efficiency [14]. Similarly, the water vapor in the aqueous phase could generate reactive oxygen species through the acoustic thermal decomposition [58]. Therefore, the free radicals could be generated in the solid phase, which could transform the adsorbed/unadsorbed tetracycline molecules into intermediates, H2O, and CO2 [14,58].

The ultrasound power is an important factor for the activation of persulfate by ultrasound-assisted carbon materials [128]. Kakavandi et al. demonstrated the degradation rate and kobs enhanced from 72.8 to 93.5% and 0.07–0.16 min−1 with the increase of ultrasound power from 20 to 70 W. The increasing ultrasonic intensity promoted the transition amount of ultrasonic energy to the whole system, resulting in the collapse of cavitation bubbles and the generation of reactive oxygen free radicals [138,139]. Moreover, the created microflows could refresh the catalyst surface with high reactivity for the generation of more free radicals [137]. In summary, the ultrasound is beneficial to improve the catalyst and degradation efficiency of carbon-activated persulfate AOPs. Unfortunately, the scale-up of the reactor and economic feasibility should be paid special attention in the practical application.

Microwave (MW) itself is also a method to activate the persulfate [140]. The microwave heating possesses shorter reaction time, lower energy consumption, higher reaction selectivity, and faster reaction speed in comparison with the conventional heating [141]. For example, the removal rate for ofloxacin reached 95.8% within only 60 min due to the presence of microwave [44]. The microwave activation could produce both thermal and non-thermal effects, contributing to the formation of SO4 •− (Eqs (35), (36)) [140]. Generally, the non-thermal effect is more important for the degradation efficiency by facilitating the electrons transfer and reactants collision [142]. Furthermore, the carbon materials could absorb microwave and generate a large number of hot spots on their surface, improving the movement of electrons. However, the thermal effect is only related to the reaction heating of the solution [142]. Liu et al. compared the degradation efficiency by using microwave heating and simple external heating to raise the system temperature up to 50℃ [44]. The degradation efficiency of the microwave heating was 80.6%, which was twofold higher than that of simple external heating (48.2%). Similar to the ultrasonic-assisted method, the microwave power is also very important. The degradation efficiency improved from 78.7% to 85.9% with the microwave power increasing from 100 to 300 W. However, the degradation rate decreased to 79.8% above 400 W. Both the degradation efficiency and production cost, especially microwave power, should be taken into consideration [44].

The ultraviolet irradiation at λ = 200–400 nm has been considered as an environmentally friendly and cost-effective method to activate persulfate by breaking the OO bond (Eqs (37), (38)) [1,143,144]. Except ultraviolet, the visible and simulated solar light can also be used in the system [145]. The previous studies demonstrate that the degradation rate and degradation efficiency would be improved by adding light in the carbon-activated persulfate AOPs [58,121,146]. Kemmoua et al. indicated that the degradation efficiency of sulfamethoxazole and kobs increased to 23.1% and 0.065 min−1 after the addition of sunlight [146]. The carbon-based catalysts could be excited by absorbing the light irradiation to form photo-induced electrons and photo-induced holes. Afterwards, the persulfate accept the photo-induced electrons and generate SO4 •− and HO• [58,145]. And the regeneration of active sites on the carbon surface by the light irradiation could also accelerate the degradation reaction [1].

According to the reaction mechanism, the hybrid systems with photocatalysis and persulfate-based AOPs can be classified into three types: photo-assisted persulfate activation, persulfate-assisted photocatalysis and concurrent photocatalysis-persulfate activation [1]. Each system has unique reaction pathway and reactive oxygen species. For the treatment of antibiotics, especially quinolone, β-lactam and tetracycline, the persulfate-assisted photocatalysis process is more suitable than others. The degradation performance is inversely proportional to the light energy. A wavelength of 260 nm with high enough energy is suitable for the production of free radicals by activating PMS, and the common light wavelength for PDS activation is 254 nm [1,121,144]. Overall, the activation of persulfate with short ultraviolet wavelength may result in rapid degradation of antibiotics at the cost of high energy consumption [121,144].

The carbon materials-activated persulfate process can also be combined with the electricity to improve their degradation efficiency towards antibiotics, during which the carbon materials are used as electrodes [9–12,23,52,147]. Song et al. reported the electrochemical activations of PDS by the MWCNT, graphite, black carbon, and GAC anodes for the degradation of sulfamethoxazole, which were 30–130 times higher than those of carbon/PDS system without any currents [12]. Firstly, the electricity can activate the persulfate to form free radicals with fast reaction speed directly [9,11,147]. Secondly, the condensed persulfate and antibiotics on the surface of AC fiber electrode promote the collisions and reactions between the antibiotic molecules and persulfate ions during the electrification stage [9]. The electrochemical regeneration of persulfate by sulfate ions on the anode endows the decomposition of persulfate in a continuous way, rather than a transient reaction (Eq (39)) [9,11]. Thirdly, the electrolysis process can oxidize the original carbon materials to produce more oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. carbonyl), which may enhance the activation of persulfate by carbon materials [148]. Fourthly, the addition of electricity can develop a nonradical pathway. For instance, Song et al. proved that the nonradical pathway accounted for 65% of the whole degradation process to remove sulfamethoxazole [12]. Finally, the PDS molecules in the activated state (PDS*) can be induced by applying current on the adsorbed PDS molecules [12]. The PDS* is responsible for the nonradical pathway. Besides, the PDS* will further decompose into HO• and SO4 •−, namely the radical pathway (Eqs (40), (41)).

The current density has a great influence on the electricity-assisted activation process by controlling the production of oxidants. In Lei’s work, the removal rate of total organic carbon increased from 79.3% to 92.6% with the current density increasing from 20 to 30 mA/cm2, and the complete mineralization was obtained at 40 mA/cm2 [10]. Notably, the increase of current density from 30 to 40 mA/cm2 only produced a small increase of 7%. The optimal current density should be tested according to individual system. The degradation of cefotaxime was restricted by the concentration of oxidants, mass transfer as well as electrode deactivation. Moreover, the side reaction increased at high current density, e.g. electrolytic hydrogen production (Eq (42)) [9].

Persulfate-based advanced oxidation technology for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater has received widespread attention. Carbon materials have been widely used for the activation of persulfate to produce active reaction species due to their low cost, recyclable and environmentally-friendly properties. In this review, the degradation of antibiotics by the carbon materials activated persulfate-based AOPs is summarized, including degradation mechanisms, roles of different carbon materials, degradation behaviors of various antibiotics, influence of operating parameters, and assisted activation techniques. Although the investigation of nonradical radical pathway is later than that of free radical pathway, it has better selectivity, milder removal condition, easier regulation and more application potential. The redox potential of free radicals is high, resulting in fast degradation rate and complete oxidation of the by-products into carbon dioxide and water, which is impossible in the nonradical radical pathway. For nonmetallic carbon materials, the main degradation mechanism is nonradical radical pathway. Metal-doped carbon materials with magnetic properties are easy to reuse and recycle. Different antibiotics have different response pathways and vulnerable attack sites. The coexisting anions could reduce the degradation efficiency of antibiotics in a free radical pathway, but display scarcely any influence on a nonradical pathway. The catalysis performance of carbon materials could be improved by combining other assisted technologies.

Although great progresses have been developed on the persulfate-based AOPs activated with carbon materials for the removal of antibiotics, the following problems still need to be solved in the future researches.

(I) Most of the current reported studies are performed by using simulated wastewater in lab rather than industrial wastewater. In a laboratory level, approximate 100 mL of antibiotic contaminated sample is targeted. However, the real wastewaters are of tons. More importantly, the real antibiotic polluted wastewater is more complicated with various coexisting organics and inorganics at different pH values. Therefore, the future researches should be carried out on the real wastewater. Additionally, the production cost of the practical application should be also evaluated with the common treatment technologies, for example Fenton and ozone.

(II) The recycling and reuse are difficult, because most of the carbon materials are powder. The magnetic carbon materials by doping metal elements could also solve the above-mentioned problem. Additionally, the catalytic efficiency of carbon materials could be improved by changing their physicochemical properties, including porous structure, graphitization degree, surface oxygen-contained groups, and surface persistent free radical.

(III) The investigations on the burgeoning carbon-based material, such as single-atom doped carbon materials, carbon quantum dots, and other frontier carbon materials, should be performed. Furthermore, the relationships of porous structure with the production of free radicals and electron transfer should be investigated, especially for specific surface area and average pore size. More importantly, the carbon-based single atom catalysts with the maximum atomic utilization efficiency, high catalytic activity and selectivity will be interesting in the future. How to synthesize carbon-based single atom catalysts in a low-cost strategy and how to maintain their cycling stability will appeal much more attention.
(IV) The effect of carbon materials and persulfate on the biological nature during the advanced oxidation process, especially for human beings, animal, and microorganisms should be investigated.
